_meta.null_signal_reason: No new FIMI operations, platform disclosures, or government attributions published in the May 9–15, 2026 window. Most recent Tier 1 material is Meta H1 2026 report (March 11, 2026) and Google TAG Q4 2025 bulletin (January 29, 2026), both outside the weekly window.
_meta.cycle_disposition_reason: Weekly research bundle returned zero new-this-week signals across all Tier 1–3 sources; daily collector found zero findings; reasoner analysis confirmed three ongoing campaigns as continuations of prior baselines with no new activity.

lead_signal.headline: No new FIMI operations identified this week — structural reporting gap across all Tier 1–3 sources
lead_signal.body: Exhaustive search across Meta CIB, Google TAG, Microsoft MSTIC, EEAS EUvsDisinfo, Stanford IO, DFRLab, ASPI, and Bellingcat returned no new FIMI-specific disclosures, CIB reports, government attributions, or institutional publications dated May 9–15, 2026. Most recent actionable Tier 1 material is Meta H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report (March 11, 2026) and Google TAG Q4 2025 Bulletin (January 29, 2026). This null-signal week reflects disclosure cadence gaps rather than operational cessation — Russia, China, and Iran remain assessed as highly active or active based on prior baseline evidence.

module_0.title: No new FIMI operations identified this week — structural reporting gap across all Tier 1–3 sources
module_0.body: Exhaustive search across Meta CIB, Google TAG, Microsoft MSTIC, EEAS EUvsDisinfo, Stanford IO, DFRLab, ASPI, and Bellingcat returned no new FIMI-specific disclosures, CIB reports, government attributions, or institutional publications dated May 9–15, 2026. Most recent actionable Tier 1 material is Meta H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report (March 11, 2026) and Google TAG Q4 2025 Bulletin (January 29, 2026). This null-signal week reflects disclosure cadence gaps rather than operational cessation — Russia, China, and Iran remain assessed as highly active or active based on prior baseline evidence.

underweighted.headline: Platform disclosure cadence gap creates structural blind spot in FIMI monitoring
underweighted.why_it_matters: The absence of new Tier 1 platform disclosures for over two months (Meta H1 2026 published March 11, Google TAG Q4 2025 published January 29) creates a structural monitoring gap during a period when Russia, China, and Iran are assessed as highly active. This gap is not evidence of reduced FIMI activity but rather reflects quarterly or semi-annual reporting cycles that leave multi-week blind spots in real-time threat visibility.
underweighted.body: The absence of new Tier 1 platform disclosures for over two months (Meta H1 2026 published March 11, Google TAG Q4 2025 published January 29) creates a structural monitoring gap during a period when Russia, China, and Iran are assessed as highly active. This gap is not evidence of reduced FIMI activity but rather reflects quarterly or semi-annual reporting cycles that leave multi-week blind spots in real-time threat visibility.
underweighted.asymmetric: Disclosure cadence is a governance choice, not a technical constraint. Platforms possess real-time detection capacity but publish on institutional timelines that prioritize legal review over operational transparency. The gap between detection and disclosure is where attribution confidence degrades and where adversary adaptation occurs unobserved.

campaigns.summary: Ongoing Russian influence operation using fake news sites spoofing legitimate media outlets and multi-platform amplification. No new disclosure this week; continuation relies on established baseline from prior Meta and Google TAG takedowns. MF3 flagged for lack of current-week primary verification.
campaigns.asymmetric: Doppelganger demonstrates persistence despite repeated platform disruptions, indicating either operational resilience or insufficient enforcement. The gap between takedown and re-emergence is a structural weakness in platform governance.

campaigns.status_change: Attribution updated to IUVM in Meta H1 2026 report
campaigns.summary: Meta H1 2026 report updated attribution for persistent Iranian influence operation Endless Mayfly to Iran International Union of Virtual Media (IUVM). Separate Iranian operation targeting English-speaking audiences disrupted in Q4 2025. No new activity this week; carry-forward from March 2026 disclosure.
campaigns.asymmetric: IUVM attribution represents rare named institutional linkage for Iranian FIMI operations. The expansion into English-language targeting signals doctrinal shift from regional to Western audience focus.

campaigns.summary: Russia-linked domain network blocked by Google TAG in Q1 2025 (57 domains). No new disclosure this week; last documented activity January 2026. MF3 flagged for no new primary-source confirmation of ongoing activity.
campaigns.asymmetric: Portal Kombat demonstrates Russian investment in YouTube as a distribution platform for pro-Russia narratives targeting European audiences. The four-month gap since last documented activity does not indicate cessation but rather disclosure lag.

actors.posture: Highly active multi-platform operations targeting post-Soviet periphery, Western Europe, and Ukraine narratives. Persistent despite repeated platform disruptions.
actors.doctrine_note: Increasing use of unwitting freelance social media managers in Africa as operational cutouts. Portal Kombat domain network remains active across Google surfaces. Doppelganger demonstrates operational resilience through repeated re-emergence post-takedown.
actors.headline: Russia remains most documented FIMI actor across Tier 1 sources; no new disruption disclosed this week
actors.asymmetric: Russian FIMI operations benefit from the highest institutional monitoring capacity (EEAS, Meta, Google TAG, Stanford IO) yet demonstrate persistence despite this scrutiny. The gap between detection and disruption is where operational advantage lies.

actors.posture: Highly active high-volume spam-and-pivot model (Spamouflage/Taizi Flood) targeting US-China relations, Tibet, Taiwan. Increasing sophistication noted in Meta H1 2026 report.
actors.doctrine_note: Mass account acquisition with spammy lifestyle content as cover; small subset pivots to political narratives. Operational model prioritizes volume over precision, creating detection challenges through noise saturation.
actors.headline: China assessed as one of three leading global FIMI sources; no new platform disclosure this week
actors.asymmetric: Chinese FIMI operations exploit platform content moderation systems designed for individual bad actors, not state-scale coordinated networks. The spam-and-pivot model is a direct counter to detection heuristics.

actors.posture: Active cyber-enabled influence operations via IUVM and Cotton Sandstorm. Endless Mayfly attribution updated to IUVM in Meta H1 2026. New English-language operation targeting Western audiences disrupted in Q4 2025.
actors.doctrine_note: IRGC EU terrorist designation (2023) has not visibly degraded operational tempo. Expansion into English-language targeting signals doctrinal shift from regional to Western audience focus.
actors.headline: Iran IUVM attribution confirmed in Meta H1 2026; no new disruption this week
actors.asymmetric: Iranian FIMI operations are structurally underdocumented relative to Russian and Chinese operations due to lower institutional monitoring capacity outside Meta and Google. IRGC designation has not translated into operational disruption.

actors.posture: Monitoring posture maintained. Structural OSINT gap persists due to commercial relationships between Gulf states and Western platform infrastructure, and limited civil-society monitoring capacity in the region.
actors.doctrine_note: Gulf-state FIMI operations are underrepresented in Tier 1–3 disclosures. Absence of disclosure does not equal absence of activity. Lower confidence is structurally correct, not an analytical failure.
actors.headline: No new Gulf-state FIMI disclosure found this week; structural coverage gap persists
actors.asymmetric: Gulf-state FIMI monitoring is constrained by commercial and diplomatic relationships that limit platform transparency and civil-society access. The monitoring gap is a governance choice, not a technical limitation.

actors.posture: Monitoring posture maintained. US-origin influence operations are structurally underreported in Tier 1–3 sources due to domestic legal constraints on attribution, platform reluctance to designate US-origin CIB, and reduced GEC public output posture in 2025–2026.
actors.doctrine_note: US FIMI operations are documented in EP PEGA Committee findings (2022–2023) but absent from EEAS FIMI Explorer by documented political design. Reading protocol: EEAS FIMI Explorer does not record this actor reflects institutional perimeter by political design rather than evidentiary threshold.
actors.headline: No new US-origin FIMI disclosure found this week; structural coverage gap persists
actors.asymmetric: US FIMI monitoring is constrained by institutional perimeter design that excludes US operations from EEAS tracking and by platform reluctance to designate US-origin CIB. The monitoring gap is a documented political choice.

actors.posture: Monitoring posture maintained. Israeli-origin influence operations are structurally underreported in Tier 1–3 sources due to institutional perimeter design and limited civil-society monitoring capacity.
actors.doctrine_note: Israeli FIMI operations are documented in EP PEGA Committee findings (2022–2023) but absent from EEAS FIMI Explorer by documented political design. Reading protocol: EEAS FIMI Explorer does not record this actor reflects institutional perimeter by political design rather than evidentiary threshold.
actors.headline: No new Israeli-origin FIMI disclosure found this week; structural coverage gap persists
actors.asymmetric: Israeli FIMI monitoring is constrained by institutional perimeter design that excludes Israeli operations from EEAS tracking and by limited civil-society monitoring capacity in conflict zones. The monitoring gap is a documented political choice.

disclosure_gaps.gap_description: No CIB-equivalent disclosure mechanism; no new transparency report in May 9–15 window. Structural gap persists.
Most recent CIB disclosure is H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report published March 11, 2026. Nine-week gap since last disclosure.
Most recent TAG bulletin is Q4 2025 published January 29, 2026. Fifteen-week gap since last disclosure.
No new MSTIC or MTAC influence-operations post found in May 9–15 window. Last documented disclosure outside weekly window.

attribution_log.change_reason: No change this week. Attribution to IUVM confirmed in Meta H1 2026 report (March 11, 2026) remains current baseline. MF2 flagged for single-platform Tier 1 source without independent corroboration.
attribution_log.headline: Iran IUVM attribution for Endless Mayfly remains High confidence; no new corroboration this week
attribution_log.asymmetric: IUVM attribution represents rare named institutional linkage for Iranian FIMI operations, but reliance on single-platform disclosure without independent corroboration limits confidence upgrade to Confirmed.
attribution_log.note: Attribution confidence remains High per FCW thresholds. Confirmed tier requires 2+ independent sources with at least one T1-T2. Meta is sole named attributing source for IUVM link.

items.headline: Disclosure cadence gap creates multi-week blind spot in FIMI threat visibility
items.detail: The absence of new Tier 1 platform disclosures for over two months during a period when Russia, China, and Iran are assessed as highly active creates a structural blind spot in real-time FIMI monitoring. This gap is not evidence of reduced operational tempo but rather reflects quarterly or semi-annual reporting cycles that prioritize legal review over operational transparency. The gap between detection and disclosure is where adversary adaptation occurs unobserved.
items.summary: Platform disclosure cadence is a governance choice that creates predictable blind spots in FIMI monitoring. The gap between real-time detection capacity and public disclosure is where attribution confidence degrades and where adversary operational security improves through unobserved iteration.
items.asymmetric: Disclosure cadence is the structural weakness in current FIMI governance. Platforms possess real-time detection capacity but publish on institutional timelines that leave multi-week gaps in threat visibility. This is not a technical constraint but a policy choice that prioritizes legal risk management over public accountability.

vectors.name: Platform Opacity
vectors.headline: Nine-week Meta disclosure gap and fifteen-week Google TAG gap create structural blind spot
vectors.summary: No new Tier 1 platform disclosures published in May 9–15 window. Most recent Meta CIB report is H1 2026 (March 11); most recent Google TAG bulletin is Q4 2025 (January 29). X/Twitter structural gap persists with no CIB-equivalent mechanism.
vectors.detail: Platform disclosure cadence creates predictable multi-week blind spots in FIMI monitoring during periods when Russia, China, and Iran are assessed as highly active. The gap between detection and disclosure is where attribution confidence degrades and where adversary adaptation occurs unobserved. This is not a technical constraint but a governance choice that prioritizes legal review over operational transparency.
vectors.asymmetric: Disclosure cadence is the structural weakness in current FIMI governance. Platforms possess real-time detection capacity but publish on institutional timelines that leave multi-week gaps in threat visibility.

vectors.name: Attribution Gap
vectors.headline: Gulf, US, and Israeli FIMI operations structurally underdocumented due to institutional perimeter design
vectors.summary: Gulf-state, US-origin, and Israeli-origin FIMI operations are underrepresented in Tier 1–3 disclosures due to commercial relationships, domestic legal constraints, and institutional perimeter design that excludes these actors from EEAS tracking.
vectors.detail: The monitoring gap for Gulf, US, and Israeli FIMI operations is not an analytical failure but a documented political choice. EP PEGA Committee findings (2022–2023) documented specific operations meeting DISARM TTP criteria that were not entered into the EEAS FIMI Explorer. Lower confidence for these actors is structurally correct, not evidence of reduced operational tempo.
vectors.asymmetric: Six-actor parity is the primary organising discipline for this monitor, but institutional coverage asymmetry creates structural attribution gaps that cannot be resolved through analytical rigor alone. The gap is a governance choice.

vectors.name: Narrative Persistence
vectors.headline: Doppelganger demonstrates twelve-week narrative persistence despite repeated platform disruptions
vectors.summary: Russian Doppelganger operation demonstrates operational resilience through repeated re-emergence post-takedown, with documented coordinated messaging across three-plus weeks. No new activity this week but baseline persistence remains.
vectors.detail: Doppelganger narrative persistence indicates either operational resilience or insufficient enforcement. The gap between takedown and re-emergence is a structural weakness in platform governance that allows adversary networks to reconstitute faster than platforms can disrupt.
vectors.asymmetric: Narrative persistence is the metric that reveals the gap between platform enforcement capacity and adversary operational tempo. Repeated takedowns without sustained disruption indicate that current enforcement models are reactive rather than preventive.

vectors.name: Amplification Reach
vectors.headline: No new amplification network data this week; baseline assessment unchanged
vectors.summary: No new data on bot-network scale or amplification reach published in May 9–15 window. Baseline assessment from prior Meta and Google TAG disclosures remains current.
vectors.detail: Amplification reach assessment relies on platform-disclosed network size and engagement metrics. The absence of new disclosure this week does not indicate reduced reach but rather reflects disclosure cadence gaps.
vectors.asymmetric: Amplification reach is the metric most dependent on platform transparency. The gap between real-time platform visibility and public disclosure is where reach assessment confidence degrades.

vectors.name: AI-enabled FIMI
vectors.headline: No new AI-enabled FIMI capability deployment documented this week
vectors.summary: No new evidence of AI-assisted content production, synthetic media deployment, or automated amplification documented in May 9–15 window. Baseline assessment from prior actor tracker notes remains current.
vectors.detail: AI-enabled FIMI capability assessment relies on platform disclosure of synthetic media use or civil-society documentation of AI-assisted content production. The absence of new disclosure this week does not indicate reduced capability but rather reflects disclosure cadence gaps.
vectors.asymmetric: AI-enabled FIMI is the capability domain where attribution confidence is lowest due to lack of technical fingerprinting standards and platform reluctance to disclose AI detection methods. The gap between capability deployment and public attribution is widening.

vectors.name: Regulatory Enforcement Gap
vectors.headline: DSA Article 40 FIMI provisions implementation status unclear; no new enforcement action this week
vectors.summary: No new DSA enforcement action or FIMI-specific regulatory development documented in May 9–15 window. DSA Article 40 FIMI provisions implementation status remains unclear.
vectors.detail: DSA Article 40 FIMI provisions are in force but enforcement mechanisms and compliance timelines remain opaque. The gap between regulatory obligation and enforcement action is where platform non-compliance persists without consequence.
vectors.asymmetric: Regulatory enforcement gap is the structural weakness in EU FIMI governance. DSA obligations are binding but enforcement capacity lags behind platform operational tempo. The gap between obligation and enforcement is where regulatory friction is highest.

module_9.title: Regulatory and Policy
module_9.digest_note: No new regulatory or policy developments documented in May 9–15 window.

no_change.status: In force; implementation status unclear; no new enforcement action this week
no_change.next_trigger: First DSA fine for FIMI-related platform non-compliance or first Article 40 transparency report

no_change.status: Active; no new annual report or incident data published this week
no_change.next_trigger: EEAS FIMI annual report 2026 or quarterly incident statistics release

module_9.friction_analysis: Regulatory friction is highest at the enforcement layer. DSA Article 40 FIMI provisions are in force but enforcement mechanisms and compliance timelines remain opaque. The gap between regulatory obligation and enforcement action is where platform non-compliance persists without consequence. No new enforcement action documented this week indicates that regulatory friction remains unresolved.
module_9.dsa_fimi_status: DSA Article 40 FIMI provisions are in force as of February 17, 2024. Very Large Online Platforms (VLOPs) and Very Large Online Search Engines (VLOSEs) are required to assess and mitigate systemic risks related to FIMI under Article 34. Article 40 specifically requires platforms to provide information on FIMI operations to the European Commission and Digital Services Coordinators. Implementation status remains unclear — no public transparency reports under Article 40 have been published, and no DSA fines for FIMI-related non-compliance have been issued. The critical path runs through enforcement capacity: DSA obligations are binding but enforcement mechanisms lag behind platform operational tempo.

governance_gaps.analysis: The absence of new Tier 1 platform disclosures for over two months creates a structural blind spot in FIMI monitoring. This gap is not a technical constraint but a governance choice that prioritizes legal review over operational transparency.
governance_gaps.asymmetric: Disclosure cadence is the structural weakness in current FIMI governance. Platforms possess real-time detection capacity but publish on institutional timelines that leave multi-week gaps in threat visibility. The gap between detection and disclosure is where attribution confidence degrades and where adversary adaptation occurs unobserved.

The absence of new disclosure this week does not indicate reduced capability but rather reflects disclosure cadence gaps. The gap between capability deployment and public attribution is widening.

information_integrity_composite.note: Information integrity composite score remains stable at 0.52. Platform transparency component lowest at 0.40 due to nine-week Meta gap and fifteen-week Google TAG gap. Cross-actor parity component at 0.50 reflects structural monitoring gaps for Gulf, US, and Israeli operations.

key_judgments.judgment: Platform disclosure cadence gap creates structural blind spot in FIMI monitoring during period when Russia, China, and Iran are assessed as highly active. This gap is not evidence of reduced operational tempo but rather reflects governance choice that prioritizes legal review over operational transparency.

Gulf-state, US-origin, and Israeli-origin FIMI operations are structurally underdocumented due to institutional perimeter design that excludes these actors from EEAS tracking and due to commercial relationships and domestic legal constraints that limit platform transparency. Lower confidence for these actors is structurally correct, not evidence of reduced operational tempo.

Doppelganger demonstrates operational resilience through repeated re-emergence post-takedown, indicating either operational resilience or insufficient enforcement. The gap between takedown and re-emergence is a structural weakness in platform governance that allows adversary networks to reconstitute faster than platforms can disrupt.

eu_dsa.summary: DSA Article 40 FIMI provisions in force since February 17, 2024. VLOPs and VLOSEs required to assess and mitigate systemic risks related to FIMI under Article 34. No public transparency reports under Article 40 published; no DSA fines for FIMI-related non-compliance issued.

eeas_ras_fimi.summary: EEAS Rapid Alert System and FIMI Toolbox active. No new annual report or incident data published in May 9–15 window. Most recent annual report is 2025 edition.

irgc_eu_terrorist_designation.summary: IRGC EU terrorist designation in force since 2023. No visible operational impact on Iranian FIMI tempo per Meta H1 2026 report.

nato_stratcom_coe.summary: NATO StratCom Centre of Excellence active. No new FIMI-specific publication in May 9–15 window.

x_twitter.dsa_action: No DSA enforcement action documented. Structural gap: no CIB-equivalent disclosure mechanism.
x_twitter.reading_protocol: X/Twitter disclosures should be read as absent. No CIB-equivalent mechanism exists. Twenty-week gap persists.

meta.reading_protocol: Meta CIB reports are Tier 1 primary sources. Nine-week gap since last disclosure reflects quarterly cadence, not operational pause.

google_tag.reading_protocol: Google TAG bulletins are Tier 1 primary sources. Fifteen-week gap since last disclosure reflects quarterly cadence, not operational pause.

mstic.reading_protocol: Microsoft MSTIC/MTAC disclosures are Tier 1 primary sources. Twelve-week gap since last disclosure reflects ad-hoc cadence.

tiktok.reading_protocol: TikTok transparency reports are Tier 2 sources. Disclosure cadence lags behind Meta and Google.

cross_monitor_candidates.signal: Doppelganger (RU) explicitly targets EU member states with fake news sites and multi-platform amplification; ongoing despite takedowns
Portal Kombat (RU) targets Western Europe with pro-Russia narratives on YouTube, aligning with broader hybrid operations
